title: AI-Enhanced Public Domain Books
objective:
I wanted to build a set of Python tools to transform, clean, and reformat public domain books using AI, so I could create modernized, cleanly structured ebooks with properly handled footnotes, headings, and metadata.
---
I wanted to build a mostly automated system that could find, clean, rewrite, and publish public domain books and documents using AI, and then package those works for sale as ebooks, audiobooks, and print books while testing different web and marketing tools along the way.
---
I wanted to build a fully automated pipeline that could transform, clean, and publish modern-language editions of classic public domain texts as ebooks, print books, and audiobooks by combining Python scripting, AI rewriting, batch processing, and publishing tools.
---
I wanted to build a scalable, professional system for producing, packaging, and selling modernized, AI-narrated public domain audiobooks, with clean branding, automated pipelines, and support for smooth digital delivery to customers.
---
I wanted to build tools and workflows for modernizing classic books, automating metadata generation, ebook formatting, audiobook production, and handling domain and email transitions for my publishing projects.
---
I wanted to build a reliable, automated pipeline to clean, enhance, and publish classic books as ebooks and marketing-ready content, making sure the process could handle tricky markdown files, metadata, covers, and marketing assets with minimal manual fixes.
summary:
I started by realizing I could use AI to transform public domain books in creative ways and decided to build a Python pipeline using the OpenAI API to preprocess and rewrite texts from Project Gutenberg. I set up a modular project, wrote initial scripts for cleaning and transforming text, and resolved issues with file handling and API key management in Windows. As I refined the cleaning process, I dealt with tricky details like paragraph breaks and modernizing language while keeping the original narrative voice. I experimented with prompt engineering to move from summarizing text to maintaining style and specific instructions, especially for novels.

I introduced a multi-pass pipeline to handle language modernization, perspective shifts, and enrichment step by step, and learned how changing temperature parameters could give me more control over creativity in each stage. I researched the legal and practical aspects of working with public domain sources and figured out how to credit original sources properly. When I ran the full Critique of Pure Reason through my pipeline, I confirmed my API cost estimates, observed issues like missing headings, and started improving my scripts to fix them.

I noticed that AI was better at non-fiction and started to research the limits of model pre-training versus fine-tuning for style and consistency, considering the requirements for collecting good example datasets. I found and explored several online communities and tools devoted to AI-assisted fiction writing, evaluating their techniques and workflows. Drawing from Sudowrite’s chunk-based approach, I improved my own chunking and metadata strategies, and calculated data usage for large-scale API calls.

I wrote scripts to unwrap hard-wrapped lines and remove or extract footnotes from Gutenberg files, making sure these could be preserved for ebook footers or appendices. After seeing issues with detecting headings in classic texts, I spent several iterations refining Python scripts using regular expressions and pattern matching to robustly identify and wrap single or multi-line headings, planning for proper markdown formatting. I set up workflows to turn cleaned markdown files into well-structured EPUBs with Pandoc, learned how to add covers, metadata, and tables of contents, and refined scripts to convert various heading formats for compatibility.

I ran into challenges with exported Google Docs files containing hidden HTML, so I wrote additional scripts to clean these out before running my heading detection. I further improved my footnote removal scripts to handle malformed and multi-line notes, ensuring clean outputs for both non-fiction and fiction pipelines. As I explored processing fiction, I developed new pipelines using multi-stage summarization and narrative reconstruction, adapting and renaming my scripts to support this two-step workflow.

By the end, I had a modular, repeatable workflow to transform and summarize public domain texts (both fiction and non-fiction), split and clean them, handle artifacts like HTML or footnotes, convert them to ebook formats with proper metadata and structure, and experiment with both straight AI rewriting and staged summarization techniques, all while learning a lot about the quirks of classic texts and AI capabilities.
---
I started by figuring out what parts of my Project Gutenberg rewriting business I liked and wanted to automate, especially the technical side. I built a local Python agent that could find, download, and clean public domain history and philosophy books from Project Gutenberg using GPT-4 lists and web scraping. After hitting limitations with some packages, I switched to using requests and BeautifulSoup for direct scraping, and cleaned up my API key handling. I updated my scripts to use the new OpenAI SDK and generated book excerpts, batch rewrote them with my pipeline, and automated side-by-side comparison files for easier review. Along the way, I refactored everything into functional modules so I could automate the whole workflow hands-free.

I realized there was a lot more to do beyond just rewriting the books. I started building scripts to generate marketing assets using GPT-4o. I learned to send only the first 3,000 words to save costs and confirmed GPT-4o handled markdown nicely. I also tweaked my comparison scripts to group and display paragraphs properly, making it easier to compare the original and rewritten versions. I put together a pipeline that handled everything from discovery and download all the way through rewriting and creating marketing materials.

I decided to add audiobooks to my offerings and researched ElevenLabs, OpenAI TTS, and Google TTS for this. I found ElevenLabs worked best for long narration with SSML, and started planning an automated workflow that added SSML markup with ChatGPT before sending it to the TTS API. When I ran into anti-bot walls scraping the Congressional Record, I switched to using Playwright to automate browser scraping, which worked after some setup headaches. Once I got clean Congressional Record TXT files from Congress.gov, I wrote scripts to combine and clean them, sometimes falling back to PDF extraction with tools like pdfplumber and regex to deal with garbled text.

On the content side, I explored rewriting not just books but also open access scientific articles, using ChatGPT to create plain-language summaries and adapting content for websites and social media. I experimented with tools like Canva and CapCut for making Instagram carousels and Reels and got a handle on what different platforms needed in terms of content styles. I realized Reels boosted my Instagram growth most, but also faced headaches with Wix's poor responsive design, so I started building my site on Shopify for more flexibility.

As I worked through options for selling digital books, I learned about Shopify apps for digital downloads, DRM, and watermarking, then realized I could launch with just basic protection and switch to DRM later. Converting markdown files for print and digital was a technical hurdle—I worked through MiKTeX and xelatex installation issues, eventually decided to use Overleaf and Pandoc for LaTeX, but then found Word simpler for basic book layout. I built Pandoc and CSS-based pipelines for generating EPUBs with styled tables of contents, had to tweak for e-reader differences, and learned that Kindle sometimes overrides my CSS choices.

Looking back, I pieced together a nearly end-to-end system that finds, cleans, rewrites, and packages old books for modern readers, with automation for most steps and lots of technical dead ends and workarounds along the way. I learned a ton about text extraction, book formatting, web scraping, marketing content, and the practical gotchas of building a digital book business.
---
I started by merging several small scripts into a unified, flexible Python tool for rewriting old texts, moving all prompts into an external JSON for easier updates. I set up batch processing to handle large numbers of Project Gutenberg books, designing the workflow to clean, format, and excerpt the original files. Early on, I debugged many formatting issues caused by inconsistent line endings, and I wrote custom scripts to reliably handle paragraph breaks and remove unwanted whitespace, footnotes, and other junk text. I carefully separated each processing stage—scraping, cleaning, and chunking for test runs—so I could fine-tune scripts with batch runners and overlays.

As the workflow matured, I developed and modularized batch apply scripts to automate running Python utilities across all files in a directory. I created overlay JSON formats and wrote scripts to apply these overlays for custom cleaning on a per-book basis, and even used LLMs with lit_operator to help classify and filter out irrelevant or structural text like chapter headings and footnotes. Template prompts went through several refinements as I learned exactly how much guidance the models needed for high-fidelity rewriting of philosophy books. I tested dozens of approaches for paragraph splitting, normalization, and junk removal, always checking formatting outputs in Notepad++ and VS Code.

I set up a publishing workflow using Markdown files with YAML metadata blocks, batch scripts for ebook and PDF production with Pandoc, and custom front-matter automation. I had to debug problems related to system binaries, environment paths, and Windows Smart App Control interfering with Pandoc and ffmpeg, switching to file renaming or command-line flags to get around platform issues. For better publishing results, I developed HTML/JS tools for visual editing and normalization of Markdown headings, letting me quickly apply batch fixes, label chapters, and prepare files for both ebook and audiobook production.

As the pipeline expanded, I planned and automated batch cover generation in InDesign by mapping book metadata to CSV files and standardizing filenames. On the audio side, I built an end-to-end script chain: normalizing text for TTS (so the output sounded natural), splitting books into per-chapter or per-section text chunks, running ElevenLabs TTS to generate mp3 or wav audio files from those chunks, and then scripting the final stitch into MP3 and M4B audiobooks with embedded metadata and chapter markers using ffmpeg. I learned first-hand how audiobook audio could be improved by merging short chunks, properly handling pauses between sections, and fixing filename length errors on Windows. Embedding cover art in the audio files led me to adjust codecs and ffmpeg flags, as I discovered limitations of different container formats.

Throughout, I created and maintained a single book metadata file in JSON that powered cover generation, ebook/PDF front-matter, and audiobook manifests. Cost and API usage estimates led me to prioritize which books to modernize and release first. I finished by generating a high-quality supporting set of metadata and CSVs, with everything named and structured for industrial-scale automation. By the end, I had built a robust pipeline that could clean, rewrite, typeset, generate covers for, and publish classic works as modern ebooks, print books, and audiobooks—controlling every step from raw text to retail-ready product.
---
I started by rethinking my cover design process, choosing templates and automation tools like Canva and Figma instead of custom illustrations to focus on consistency and genre branding. I cleaned and reformatted my book metadata in CSV files and organized filenames to match asset and text files for seamless automation, which set up a solid foundation for bulk processing later on. Early in the process, I discovered that exporting files with custom names in Canva was not possible, so I wrote a Python script to automate cover image renaming and align all media with my publishing manifest.

Once my asset pipeline was reliable, I turned to selling and distributing audiobooks. I concluded that Shopify, not Audible, was best for direct sales, bundles, pricing control, and customer relationships. I picked Sky Pilot as my delivery app for secure digital fulfillment and bundles. I did pricing research and structured my catalog around two main price tiers, using bundles and multi-format delivery to drive value and fight piracy with customer goodwill instead of heavy DRM. Alongside, I honed my cover and branding strategy with SVG logos, custom fonts, and clear branding, experimenting in Canva, Figma, and Illustrator.

On the production side, I spent a lot of time experimenting with AI text-to-speech voices and pipelines. I built, tested, and refined Python scripts to process texts, wrap paragraphs in SSML prosody tags, chunk content, and send batches to ElevenLabs, OpenAI, and Azure TTS APIs. I compared costs and audio quality, discovering significant price differences and subtle model quirks—like Azure's distinct voice naming and OpenAI's expressiveness limits. For narration, I analyzed how pause tags, prosody, and chunking rhythm affected the actual listening experience, tuning my scripts and prompts for a more natural, philosophical pace.

Building the audiobook files was another technical challenge. I stitched audio chunks into MP3 and M4B files using Python and ffmpeg, iterating repeatedly to fix issues with chapters, metadata, bitrate, normalization, and file size. I learned how to use loudnorm, compressor filters, and even forced the RF64 format in ffmpeg to process long books without breaking the WAV file limit. When I hit bugs—like missing chapters or bad XML in SSML—I updated my chunking, manifest, and stitching scripts for robustness. I realized how important it was to produce clean chapters from Markdown headings, so I rewrote my chunker to extract and preserve real section titles and mapped these reliably throughout the manifest and the audio pipeline.

Through trial and error, I set up automation scripts for inserting SSML breaks, handling code blocks in Markdown, and customizing pauses for a deliberate narration style. I patched and improved my cover processing by baking in cover attachment to the final audio output, again using ffmpeg and adjusting to workflows that required as little manual intervention as possible. Along the way, I documented command-line usage examples, troubleshoot environment variable setup with PowerShell for cloud APIs, and made everything modular so I could build, test, and repeat the process for new books easily.

Toward the end, I expanded beyond audiobooks to explore making philosophical video essays—with animation overlays instead of plain talking heads—using new AI avatar tools. I reviewed feasibility based on computing limitations, but ultimately focused on refining my main pipeline, especially the audio post-processing and chapter metadata. By the close of the conversation, I had a nearly end-to-end automated system: manuscript to chunked, SSML-enhanced text, to batch TTS generation with expressive and brand-coherent narration, to professionally normalized and chaptered audio files with covers, ready for upload and digital delivery. I felt good about the mix of automation and handcrafted creative decisions, and clear about next steps for expanding the catalog or adding video features.
---
I started by organizing public domain works into themed reading bundles, planning essay series and courses, and refining reading lists using Project Gutenberg. I wrote code and built web tools, like a Roman Numeral Normalizer, and continually improved my Markdown and audio processing scripts to handle automation, chunking, and batch processing for both text and audio. I tweaked my workflow to ensure accurate book metadata and experimented with batch-injecting YAML front matter and modern title pages into markdown files, sometimes having to troubleshoot file format, duplications, and cleaning up artifacts.

For audiobooks, I designed and documented complete pipelines for splitting, chunking, text-to-speech, loudness normalization, and batch stitching using Python, FFmpeg, and Azure TTS. I dealt with tricky metadata, cover images, chapter info, and streamlined the scripts into robust batch processes. Along the way, I hit obstacles like large file problems in git, which led to deep cleaning my repo with git-filter-repo, and learned to manage product listings in Shopify by scripting CSV edits, setting up collections and smart tags, and refining my store design for easy navigation.

As my project grew, I expanded my audio pipelines to allow more advanced narration using AI voice tools, and tackled technical migrations by moving domains and email from Wix to Namecheap, keeping Google Workspace working mid-transfer and ensuring my email and DNS records stayed safe. I documented the step-by-step context and continually updated configurations, pushing new files to my repos and working through challenges with push and branch settings.
---
I started by realizing that cleaning up markdown files before ebook conversion required a robust, multi-layer approach, so I built a local HTML/JS markdown cleanup editor combining detection, editing, and keyboard-controlled fixes, refactoring my older Python logic into JavaScript. Along the way, I tackled edge cases like bilingual books and YAML front matter bugs, refining scripts and adding preprocessing filters for special cases, while learning to validate YAML and debug errors with Pandoc.

For images, I wrote and iterated on several Python scripts to fetch author images from Wikimedia Commons, making them more resilient to name mismatches and adding overrides and better fallback logic. I built functions to select and filter images, exclude unwanted ones, and keep a manifest for easy retrieval later. When Shopify imports failed due to small errors, I fixed the output logic and streamlined the CSV generator before moving on to organizing the history and architecture of my Anti-Prince idea transformation pipeline, realizing I could standardize the flow with manifest-driven automation for both main and derivative pipelines.

I evolved my process to extract and expand principles from classic texts, creating specialized scripts to batch-clean and split texts into semantic blocks, wire up YAML-based automation, and set up batch wrappers for scalable principle extraction. To handle the flood of extracted principles, I built attentive scoring and filtering scripts, combining deterministic logic with LLM-based ranking and reasoning, then used local HTML/JS viewers and automated selectors to winnow down hundreds of entries to the most interesting and marketable. Getting the AI prompts right was a challenge—I rewrote instructions multiple times to push for sharper, less generic outputs and improved scoring for creativity, originality, and audience appeal.

Once I had robust JSON outputs, I wrote Python scripts to merge, flatten, and render these as clean Markdown and later as attractive PDFs. I designed and iterated new prompt and pipeline structures so that expanded principles had richer explanations, real-world examples, and marketing hooks. For marketing, I turned the whole principle pipeline into a multi-product engine, generating CSVs for Canva Bulk Create, Buffer scheduling, and Substack drafts, streamlining tags and calendar formats for easier automation and initial manual QA.

As the book and marketing lines grew, I adapted the ebook pipeline to companion books, reusing and duplicating cover images, metadata, and selection files. I modified scripts to handle new directory layouts, patched cover and metadata injectors, and set up a YAML-driven workflow to produce PDFs, EPUBs, covers, and all the marketing collateral from the same sources. At every stage I fixed path and formatting bugs—like YAML indentation and heading wrapper issues—while repeatedly updating scripts to ensure correct author display, clean TOC levels, and stable file structures.

Toward the end, I integrated ReportLab for more attractive PDF handouts, fixed layout and spacing, and wrote marketing copy directly into the asset pipeline. I learned to patch pipelines manually when JSON cover selection files got stale and automated only after thorough manual checks ensured nothing would silently break. By the end, I had a repeatable, modular pipeline that could take cleaned markdown through expansion, scoring, rendering, and asset creation, all the way to ready-to-publish ebooks, companion books, marketing images, and CSVs for Shopify and social media publishing.
